There used to be a market day every tenday and the whole settlement turned out for it
The old wall was twice as tall before the last bad winter brought half of it down
People used to leave their doors unlatched at night and nobody thought twice about it
The road to the neighboring settlement was well-kept once and you could make the trip in half a day
There was a bell in the watchtower that could be heard for miles but it cracked years ago and was never replaced
The founders built the first structures to last and those walls are still the strongest ones standing
Gatherings used to draw people from three settlements over but fewer make the journey each year
The old quartermaster kept records of every trade that passed through and those ledgers are still in the archive
Children used to play in the ruins before the council declared them off limits after the collapse
The aqueduct carried water from the high ground for generations before the stonework gave out
There was a time when every household kept a lantern burning at the gate as a welcome to travelers
The crafters guild used to set standards that kept the quality high but it dissolved after the last master retired
The settlement square used to have a fountain. You can still see where the basin was if you know where to look
There was a time when the watch had twice the numbers it does now. Budget cuts, they said
The old road markers were carved from stone and lasted generations. The wooden ones we use now rot within a few years
I remember when the market had a dozen stalls. Now it is half that on a good day
The healer's garden used to take up the entire south corner. Most of it is storage now
There was an orchard outside the walls that supplied the whole settlement. The blight took it in a single season
The smithy used to have three forges running. Now there is barely enough work for one
People used to trade by barter here. When coins came in, something changed about how neighbors dealt with each other
The old well in the square was capped years ago. They said the water went bad but nobody ever tested it properly
There used to be a night market during the warm season. Lanterns strung up across the square. It was something to see
The original settlement charter is still in the archive. Most people have never read it
The bridge used to be wide enough for two carts to pass. After it was rebuilt, they made it narrower to save on stone
I remember when the storehouse was always full to the rafters. These days we keep it half stocked at best
The old trail through the hills was the main route before the road was built. Some of the markers are still there
There was a tradition of ringing the bell at dawn and dusk. Stopped when the bell cracked and nobody revived it
The herb garden behind the hall was planted by the first healer. Some of those plants are older than anyone living here
The craftsmen used to mark everything they made with a personal stamp. You could tell who made a piece at a glance
The settlement used to host travelers regularly. There was an inn and a stable. Both are gone now
The council used to meet in the open air so everyone could hear. Now they meet behind closed doors
I remember the old lamplighter. Made the rounds every evening without fail for thirty years. Nobody replaced him when he passed
There was a system of drainage channels built into the original streets. Half of them are blocked now and nobody maintains them
The timber frame on the hall is original. You can still see the joinery marks from the first builders
The map room in the archive used to be open to everyone. They locked it after some of the charts went missing
There used to be a communal bread oven that was lit every morning. People would gather while they waited. It was the heart of the settlement
The old watchtower had a signal fire platform at the top. The whole network is abandoned now
I remember when the craftsmen took apprentices from every family. Now they only teach their own
The storeroom under the hall was a meeting place during the hard times. You can still see where people carved their names into the walls
There was a ferry crossing downriver that connected us to the southern trade route. The operator moved on and nobody took over
The hunting grounds used to be open to everyone. Then the council restricted access and half the settlement resented it
The old stonemason carved a sundial into the courtyard that still keeps accurate time if you know how to read it
There was a shared kiln outside the walls that anyone could use. It collapsed and was never rebuilt
I remember when every child learned basic survival skills before they were ten. Not sure that happens anymore
The settlement used to have a proper armory. Most of the equipment was sold off during the lean years
There was a standing agreement with the neighboring settlement about mutual aid. I do not think either side honors it anymore
The old granary could hold enough to feed the settlement through two bad seasons. The new one holds about half that
The woodworker who built the hall doors carved them with scenes from the founding. The detail is finer than anything we could manage now
There used to be a storyteller who visited every season. The children would gather and the adults would find excuses to listen too
The irrigation channels were dug by hand over the course of three generations. We use less than a third of them now
I remember when the path to the creek was lined with torches every evening. Felt safer somehow
The old wall had watchtowers at every corner. Only two are still standing and one of those is not really usable
There was a tradition of planting a tree when a child was born. The grove behind the hall is the proof of it
The traders used to stay for days and there was a whole quarter set aside for them. Now they come and go in an afternoon
I remember the old rope bridge across the gorge. Terrifying to cross but it was the fastest way to the highlands
The metalwork on the original gates was done by a smith whose name is on the lintel. Nobody since has matched the quality
There was a time when we knew every person in the settlement by name. The place has grown past that
The common room in the hall used to have a fire burning day and night, tended in shifts. The hearth has been cold for years
The old trade agreements were written on hide and sealed with wax. Some of them are still binding, technically
I remember when the festival lasted three full days. Now we are lucky to get an afternoon
The quarry that supplied our building stone was abandoned after the accident. We import everything now at twice the cost
There used to be a code among the craftsmen about pricing. Fair rates, no undercutting. That understanding eroded over time
The founder's monument in the square has weathered so badly you can barely read the inscription
I remember the first time the settlement held a formal election. Messy, but people felt like it mattered
The original boundary markers were set by the founders and most of them are buried or missing now
There was a windmill on the ridge that ground grain for the whole area. The mechanism seized and nobody had the skill to fix it
The communal stores used to operate on an honor system. It worked until it did not
I remember when the night sky was clear enough to navigate by. The smoke from the forges has changed that
The old cistern under the square held enough water for a month. It cracked during the dry season and was never sealed properly
There used to be a signal post on the hill that could relay messages to the next settlement in minutes. The system fell apart when the middle post was abandoned
The root cellars under the original buildings are still the best storage in the settlement. They knew how to build for the climate
I remember when the council had seven seats and every district was represented. Now there are four and half the settlement feels ignored
The old docks were built with ironwood that lasted a hundred years. The replacements are already showing rot
There was a time when a handshake sealed a deal here. Now everything needs to be written and witnessed
The training ground used to be in active use every morning. Now it is mostly storage and the younger generation has not learned half of what they should
I remember the traveling smith who came through once a year with tools and techniques from distant settlements. Nobody has filled that role since
The settlement archive has records going back to the founding but the oldest ones are in a script nobody reads fluently anymore
There was a cooperative garden that fed the families who could not manage their own plots. It was divided into private lots years ago
The beacon fire on the ridge has not been lit in a generation. I am not sure anyone remembers the protocol
I remember when disputes were settled by the elders in the open. Public, fair, final. The current system feels less certain
The original well was dug so deep it never ran dry. The newer wells do not go half as far down
There used to be an exchange system where families traded labor instead of goods. More practical in some ways
The watchtower stairs were carved from a single piece of stone. The craftsmanship alone is worth seeing
I remember when news traveled on foot and a visitor from another settlement was an event. Now traders come and go and nobody blinks
The old fishery downstream supplied the settlement reliably until the water changed and the fish moved on
There was a custom of gifting bread to new arrivals. Simple gesture but it made people feel welcome. I have not seen it in years
The original road was paved with flat stones fitted so tightly you could not slip a blade between them
I remember the master carpenter who could look at a tree and tell you exactly what it would yield. That kind of knowledge takes a lifetime to build
The communal laundry used to be the place where all the news was exchanged. It still is, but the building is falling apart
There was a beekeeper who maintained hives along the wall and supplied the whole settlement with honey. The hives were abandoned when she left
The original gatehouse had a second story with quarters for the watch. The upper floor was removed after the supports weakened
I remember when the harvest festival brought in people from so far away that we heard languages nobody here could speak
The old reservoir was lined with clay and sealed with pitch. It held water perfectly. The new one leaks every season
There used to be a post rider who carried messages between settlements on a regular schedule. The route was discontinued when the funding ran out
The first families planted windbreaks around the settlement that are now the tallest trees for miles. They planned ahead
I remember the year the whole settlement came together to rebuild after the fire. That kind of cooperation is harder to find now
The trading hall used to have a record board where every transaction was posted publicly. Kept everyone honest
There was a night patrol route that covered every corner of the settlement. It has been shortened twice and still feels inadequate
The old cemetery has markers going back to the first generation. Some of the names are the same ones on the buildings
I remember when the apprentice trials were a public event. The whole settlement would turn out to watch
The herb drying racks that used to line the south wall are mostly gone. The healer manages with less now
There was a time when every building had a rain barrel and the water was shared freely. Half the barrels have been claimed as private
The old granary floor was made of a stone that stayed cool even in the heat. The grain kept better in those days
I remember the elder who could settle any argument with a word. Since they passed, the council has not been the same
The tradition of marking the solstice with a communal meal has not been observed in three years. Nobody organized it and nobody objected to its absence
There used to be a lending library of sorts in the archive. Books and scrolls that anyone could borrow. Most of the collection has been locked away now
The watchfires used to burn all night, every night. Now they are lit only when there is a specific concern
I remember when the smith's workshop was the warmest place in the settlement during winter. People would find excuses to visit just to stand near the forge
The old patrol dogs were trained so well they could run the perimeter without a handler. Nobody breeds them that way anymore
There used to be a system of colored flags for signaling between the watch posts. Simple but effective. Lost knowledge now
I remember when every meal at the communal table started with a moment of gratitude. It fell out of practice gradually
The original stone-lined paths drained perfectly in any weather. The gravel replacements turn to mud
There was a time when the settlement sent a delegation to every neighboring gathering. Now we barely manage our own
The old root cellar under the founder's house kept food cold enough to last through the hottest months. Nothing we build now matches it
I remember when the morning horn meant something. Everyone stopped what they were doing. Now most people sleep through it
The weaving tradition here used to be famous enough that traders came specifically for the cloth. That reputation has faded
There was a mentorship custom where every established household took in a young person to teach. The last pairing was years ago
The old timber bridge was built without a single nail. Joinery alone held it for decades. The replacement uses iron at every joint
I remember the year the whole settlement planted trees along the boundary. Those trees are grown now but the spirit of cooperation has not been matched since
